front: Building: Nasrid palace complex in Granada, Spain; name derives from Arabic for 'the Red One,' referring to its red clay construction
Building: built by the Nasrid dynasty for the Muslim rulers of al-Andalus; overlooks the city of Granada
Building: adjacent to the Generalife gardens; inspired the Mudejar fusion of Islamic and Christian styles after the Reconquista
Building: Washington Irving lived here and wrote Tales from the Alhambra (1832), including 'The Legend of the Arabian Astrologer'
Building: Duke of Wellington planted English elms in this palace complex during the Peninsular War
Building: Charles V built a Renaissance palace within this complex; Moorish laborers were expelled during its over-100-year construction
Building: Marie Menken dedicated a 1961 experimental short film shot here to Kenneth Anger
Building: courtyard whose central fountain basin rests on twelve marble lion statues; has four projecting pavilions facing each other
work: Court of the Lions (Patio de los Leones)
Court of the Lions (Patio de los Leones)

front: Building: Hall of the Two Sisters and Hall of the Abencerrages are two rooms with exceptionally elaborate muqarnas domes positioned across from each other
work: Court of the Lions (Patio de los Leones)

front: Building: Hall named for its ceiling's resemblance to the hull of a boat; connected by a double arch to the Hall of the Two Sisters
work: Court of the Lions (Patio de los Leones)

front: Building: Ibn Zamrak composed inscriptions on its lion fountain comparing it to 'houses of the constellations'
work: Court of the Lions (Patio de los Leones)

front: Building: a fountain inscription reads 'the lover who in vain / tries to hide his tears from his beloved'; scholars Taabba and Grabar debated whether inscriptions relate to the Muztallite-Asharite controversy
work: Court of the Lions (Patio de los Leones)

front: Building: courtyard with a large rectangular reflecting pool (alberca) lined with myrtle shrubs; arcades run along one end
work: Court of the Myrtles (Patio de los Arrayanes)
Court of the Myrtles (Patio de los Arrayanes)

front: Building: its Court of the Myrtles lies to the south of this tower, which contains a large Hall of the Ambassadors
work: Court of the Myrtles (Patio de los Arrayanes)

front: Building: Gate of Justice, built in the horseshoe arch style, leads to a patio of myrtles surrounded by arcades with stucco sebka designs
work: Court of the Myrtles (Patio de los Arrayanes)

front: Building: contains the most intricately patterned muqarnas (honeycomb stalactite vaulting, composed of small cells called alveoles) in Islamic architecture
Building: M.C. Escher visited in 1936 and copied its tessellations, inspiring his lifelong obsession with the regular division of the plane
Building: its geometric tile decorations uniquely employ all seventeen wallpaper groups
Building: Owen Jones made innovative chromolithographs of its wall decorations that influenced his interiors for the Crystal Palace
Building: niches once held large blue-and-gold vases unique in the region for being purely decorative
Building: Francisco Tárrega's tremolo guitar etude named 'Memories of' this place, in which the i, m, a fingers play melody while the thumb plays a counter-melody
